nativeRange.habitat: Inhabits coral-rich fringing reefs in tropical marine environments; tends to swim in open water around reef structures

diet.notes: Has been reported to feed on a wide variety of hard-shelled prey, including shellfish, crustaceans, clams, and snails, with some grazing on algae and sponges noted from wild observations. In captivity, a varied diet of meaty foods such as squid, krill, clams, shrimp, and other hard-shelled items is generally recommended to help wear down the continuously growing fused beak-like teeth. Multiple small feedings per day tend to be advised over one large meal.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso (delso.photo), 4601x3067. Striking sharp lateral profile against deep blue water; clearly shows the black eye 'mask', dark pectoral-base patch and dark lips on a vibrant live Red Sea individual.
Wikimedia Commons contributor, 1910x1421. Crisp full side view over a coral reef with all fins spread; classic catalog angle showing the eye mask and dark pectoral spot.
Diego Delso (delso.photo), 1689x2533. Sharp top-down/front angle showing the paired dark eye masks distinctly; included for angle variety.

summary: The Panda Puffer (Arothron diadematus) is a Red Sea endemic pufferfish recognised by the distinctive black 'mask' around each eye. Generally considered intermediate-level; best kept in a large fish-only system with peaceful but appropriately sized tankmates.
careNotes:
The Panda Puffer is a Red Sea endemic closely related to the more widespread Indo-Pacific Dogface Puffer (Arothron nigropunctatus) and is sometimes traded as a 'Panda Dogface Puffer'. It tends to grow to around 30 cm total length and is generally considered an intermediate-level pufferfish suited to a spacious fish-only system rather than a reef tank.

Diet and dental care: Like other Arothron species, the Panda Puffer has a fused beak-like dental plate that grows continuously and must be worn down with hard-shelled foods — clams, mussels in the shell, hard-shelled shrimp, and snails. Small, frequent feedings tend to be recommended over one large daily meal.

Reef compatibility: The Panda Puffer is generally not considered reef-safe. It has been known to eat ornamental shrimp, crabs, snails, urchins, sea stars, tridacnid clams, and tubeworms, and may nip at corals and sponges.

Handling and inflation: When threatened, the Panda Puffer inflates its body as a defence. Inflation with air — which can occur when a fish is lifted out of water on a net — can be harmful, so transfers are best carried out using a sealed container submerged in water rather than a conventional net.

Toxicity: The flesh is reported to be poisonous to eat due to tetrodotoxin concentrated in the skin and viscera, but the Panda Puffer is not generally reported to release skin toxins capable of harming tankmates in the way boxfishes can.
breedingNotes: The Panda Puffer is an egg-laying species; detailed spawning behaviour and captive breeding are not well documented. Captive reproduction of Arothron pufferfishes is generally considered very rare.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care-level (Moderate), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), reef-safe (No), water parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), diet, and 180-gallon minimum tank size. Stated adult size of 13 inches appears inconsistent with FishBase's 30 cm TL.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Tetraodontidae, order Tetraodontiformes), max size (30 cm TL), temperature range (24.8-29.4°C), trophic level (3.4, omnivorous), reproduction (oviparous), distribution (Western Indian Ocean: Red Sea, endemic), and IUCN status (Least Concern).

additional.name: FreshMarine
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature (72-78°F), pH (8.1-8.4), salinity (1.020-1.025), care level (Moderate), temperament (Semi-aggressive), and diet. Cites 100-gallon minimum tank (vs LiveAquaria's 180). Native range incorrectly stated as Indo-Pacific; FishBase Red Sea was preferred.

additional.name: Tropical Marine Centre
additional.notes: Confirmed Red Sea origin and 'fish-only, roomy tank' recommendation. Lists care level as Beginner — outlier vs LiveAquaria/FreshMarine's Moderate; intermediate retained per cross-source consensus.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-laying; captive breeding is rarely if ever documented for Arothron pufferfishes.
